data.title: Regions Financial targets 3%–5% net interest income growth in 2025 as deposit momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regions Financial Corporation (RF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John M. Turner, President, CEO & Chairman, opened by highlighting "strong quarterly earnings of $534 million, resulting in earnings per share of $0.59" and noted adjusted earnings of $0.60 per share. Turner reported "pretax pre-provision income of $832 million, a 14% increase year-over-year," and a return on tangible common equity of 19%. He emphasized deposit growth across all eight priority markets and organic deposit growth of over 30% in five years. Turner added, "We grew ending loans in both the consumer and corporate bank," and referenced over 300 new commercial relationships year-to-date. He cited "record fee income" in wealth management and treasury management revenue up 8% year-to-date. Turner also announced a 6% increase in the common dividend and ongoing investments in technology, including a new mobile app rollout and a planned cloud-based deposit system conversion starting in late 2026.
  • CFO David Jackson Turner stated, "Ending loans grew 1%, while average loans remained stable," driven by C&I and real estate. He highlighted consumer deposits in priority markets growing 20% more than core markets and noted, "Interest-bearing deposit costs continued to decline as expected to 1.39%." Turner said net interest income "rebounded increasing by 5% linked quarter" and expects full-year 2025 net interest income to grow "between 3% and 5%." He also commented, "Adjusted noninterest income increased 5% linked quarter," and projected full-year 2025 adjusted noninterest income growth between 2.5% and 3.5%. Turner mentioned that full-year adjusted noninterest expense is now expected to be up 1% to 2%.

Outlook

  • Management now expects full-year 2025 average loans to be stable to up modestly versus 2024. Net interest income is projected to grow between 3% and 5% for the full year 2025. Adjusted noninterest income is expected to rise between 2.5% and 3.5% versus 2024. Adjusted noninterest expense guidance is for a 1% to 2% increase in 2025, with anticipated full-year positive operating leverage in the 150 to 250 basis point range. The net interest margin is expected to remain in the low to mid-3.60s for the remainder of the year, with potential to rise into 2026.

Financial Results

  • Regions reported $534 million in net income and $0.59 in earnings per share, with adjusted figures at $538 million and $0.60 per share. Pretax pre-provision income reached $832 million. Return on tangible common equity stood at 19%. Ending loans grew 1%, while average loans were stable. Treasury management revenue increased 8% year-to-date, and wealth management produced another quarter of record fee income. Net interest income rose 5% linked quarter. Adjusted noninterest income also climbed 5% linked quarter, driven by mortgage and card/ATM fees, as well as a record in wealth management income. Adjusted noninterest expense increased 4% compared to the prior quarter, with full-time equivalent headcount rising by just over 100 associates. The allowance for credit loss ratio declined to 1.80%, and annualized net charge-offs as a percentage of average loans decreased to 47 basis points. The common equity Tier 1 ratio ended at 10.7%.

Q&A

  • Ebrahim Huseini Poonawala, Bank of America: Asked about the impact of the recent tax bill and implications for loan growth and customer sentiment. John M. Turner responded that sentiment for business customers "hasn't changed much since we were last together 90 days ago" but that "the passage of the big beautiful bill and tax package does create some certainty," expecting "positive momentum in the second half of 2025 and 2026." Poonawala followed up on M&A strategy; Turner stated, "We are not interested in depository M&A...we are not today interested in bank M&A."
  • Robert Scott Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about margin improvement. CFO Turner explained, "We had nice growth in the margin this quarter...a couple of things that aren't going to repeat...that helped us call that $5 million, $6 million." Siefers asked about deposit cost leverage; Turner replied, "Our interest-bearing deposit costs came down and we continue to grow deposits in all of our priority markets."
  • John G. Pancari, Evercore ISI: Sought detail on loan growth drivers. Turner described pipelines as "up 17% year-over-year and 30% over the first quarter" with production "up 19% versus the quarter and 15% year-over-year." Pancari also asked about competition; Turner acknowledged competition but emphasized relationship growth and brand strength.
  • Ken Usdin, Autonomous Research: Asked about technology investments. Turner said, "We believe...we really will have a competitive advantage with respect to technology." CFO Turner added that "over time that technology costs will increase," but efficiency gains are expected.
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Questioned sustainability of noninterest-bearing deposit mix. CFO Turner explained, "We can continue to maintain that low 30% noninterest-bearing deposit account" due to client growth and focus on operating accounts. Alexopoulos also asked about loan growth versus peers; Turner noted disciplined risk-adjusted returns and reshaping the portfolio for soundness and profitability.
  • Gerard Sean Cassidy, RBC: Asked about credit quality and specific portfolios. Turner responded, "We're continuing to work through a handful of credits in the office space...transportation side, that sector continues to be stressed."
  • Betsy Lynn Graseck, Morgan Stanley: Asked about operating leverage sustainability. Turner replied, "We're committed to delivering positive operating leverage over time."
  • Matthew Derek O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Queried about commercial runoff; Turner said, "About $400 million to $500 million probably is our target" for the remainder of the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on sustainability of growth, credit quality, and the impact of technology investments. Skepticism surfaced around noninterest-bearing deposit sustainability and competitive pressures, but no overtly negative tone was present.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, frequently citing momentum and execution. In Q&A, their responses were measured and detailed, with Turner emphasizing consistency and strategic discipline. The tone was slightly more assertive and optimistic compared to the prior quarter, with fewer expressions of uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management displayed increased confidence about loan growth, deposit momentum, and technology-driven efficiency, while analysts remained cautious but less focused on macro risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net interest income was raised to 3%–5% growth, from a prior range of 1%–4%. Operating leverage guidance increased to 150–250 basis points, up from 50–150 basis points previously. Loan growth outlook improved from stable to stable-to-up. Management emphasized accelerating deposit growth, particularly in priority markets, and stronger pipelines in commercial lending. Analysts' focus shifted from macroeconomic uncertainty and tariff impact to questions about operational execution, technology investment, and competitive positioning. Management tone moved from cautious optimism to more pronounced confidence, especially regarding the bank's strategy and execution. Analysts' tone shifted from uncertainty about loan growth and credit to a more constructive stance on the sustainability of returns and competitive advantage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged a handful of credits in the office and transportation sectors as ongoing risk areas, with Turner stating, "We're continuing to work through a handful of credits in the office space...transportation side, that sector continues to be stressed." Allowance for credit loss remains at 1.80%, with net charge-offs expected toward the higher end of the 40–50 basis points range. Management reiterated discipline in portfolio management and reserved for anticipated losses. Analyst concerns focused on the sustainability of deposit growth and margin, the competitive environment in core markets, and the timeline for technology-driven efficiency gains.

Final Takeaway

Regions Financial delivered strong second quarter results with record fee income, robust deposit and loan growth in priority markets, and rising net interest income. Management signaled greater confidence in full-year growth targets and operating leverage, supported by strategic investments in technology and disciplined risk management, while addressing ongoing credit and competitive risks. The bank expects positive momentum to continue into the second half of 2025 and beyond, reflecting progress on its strategic priorities and shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sarepta plunges as FDA is reportedly evaluating if gene therapy should remain on market
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) shares dropped to a new 52-week low on Friday, triggering a trading halt after Bloomberg News reported that the FDA is reviewing whether the company’s gene therapy Elevidys, developed with Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), should remain on the market.

During an interview on Friday, FDA commissioner Marty Makary said that he is “taking a hard look” at whether Sarepta (NASDAQ:SRPT) should continue to market Elevidys, which was linked to two patient deaths this year.

His remarks came after multiple news outlets reported on Thursday that a third patient who received a different gene therapy developed by the company for limb-girdle muscular dystrophy died from liver failure in a late-stage trial.

Elevidys, approved by the FDA in 2024 for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, generated nearly $375M in net product revenue for Sarepta (NASDAQ:SRPT) in Q1 2025. In response to patient deaths, the company said this week that it has agreed with the FDA to add a black box warning to the Elevidys label.

data.title: Trump threatens 15%-20% minimum tariff in any EU deal - report
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump is seeking to impose a new baseline tariff of 15%-20% in trade talks with the European Union, rejecting the lower 10% framework that had underpinned weeks of negotiations, the Financial Times reported, citing three people familiar with the matter.

Even if a deal is struck, the Trump administration is now aiming to lock in tariffs above 10%, a U.S. official told the FT.

That effectively leaves the bloc cornered just weeks ahead of an Aug. 1 deadline, when Trump has threatened to slap a 30% levy on all EU imports. 

If Trump holds firm on locking in permanent 15%-20% duties, the outcome would mirror the starting point of negotiations back in April.

Trump has brushed off the EU's latest offer to cut car tariffs and appears content to leave the sector facing 25% duties, as originally planned, the newspaper said, citing people familiar. 

The White House did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Merck KGaA gets EU nod for Ezmekly
data.description:
• Merck KGaA (NYSE:MRK) said that the European Commission approved Ezmekly for treating symptomatic, inoperable plexiform neurofibromas in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 who are 2 years old and older.
  • The approval was given to SpringWorks Therapeutics, a healthcare company associated with Merck.

data.title: S&P, Nasdaq seesaw after new record highs; 3M, AmEx weigh on Dow
data.description:
Just a day after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq climbed into record territory, both indexes notched fresh intraday highs on Friday. However, weakness in American Express (AXP) and 3M (MMM) dragged on the Dow, leaving markets muted as a whole.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is near even while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) lags with a 0.4% decline. Meanwhile, the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is also near flat.

Adding to market uncertainty, The Financial Times reported that President Trump is pushing for a 15% to 20% minimum tariff on all imports from the European Union, reigniting trade and political tensions.

As the S&P and Nasdaq top record highs yet again, Ryan Detrick of Carson Group stated “Number nine! Today was the 9th new all-time high of 2025 for the S&P 500. Since 1957 (when it became 500 stocks) today was ATH number 1,250. Congrats to those who followed their investment plan back in April.” 

Of the 11 sectors within the S&P 500, five are trading in positive territory, led by strong gains in Utilities and Consumer Discretionary. Meanwhile, Energy lags behind, posting the steepest losses among the group. 

U.S. Treasury yields pushed lower as the shorter-end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) has dipped 4 basis points to 3.86% and the longer-end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) has fallen 3 basis points to 4.42%. 

On the economic front, building permits and housing starts rose more than expected in June. Furthermore, the U.S. consumer sentiment gauge edged up to 61.8 in July from 60.7 in June, slightly exceeding the 61.4 consensus according to data released by the University of Michigan on Friday. 

“It was a goldilocks week for economic data with most inflation readings coming in weaker than expected and everything else coming in better than expected. It wasn't quite an ‘immaculate’ week, but it was close,” Bespoke Investment said. 

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Netflix (NFLX) are lower by more than 5% after it reported its latest quarterly earning data after the bell on Thursday.

data.title: Wall Street and SA analysts weigh in on Netflix's Q2 report
data.description:
Netflix's (NASDAQ:NFLX) second-quarter earnings beat expectations on top and bottom lines, and the streaming giant raised full-year revenue expectations. Shares of the company were down nearly 5% by midday on Friday.

Word on the Street

Morgan Stanley (reiterate "overweight"; PT hiked to $1,500 from $1,450): The research firm said results were ahead of expectations. Netflix's first advertising upfront with its own ad tech stack appears a success, sustaining premium CTV ad rates and supporting a doubling of ad revs in '25. 

GenAI tools are contributing to content innovation, notably in VFX and de-aging, while Netflix is piloting a natural language UI tool. Engagement is stable YoY on a per-owner HH basis, MS analysts said on Friday.

While limited in specifics, overall momentum in the still nascent advertising business appears strong, they said.

Wells Fargo (reiterate "overweight"; PT hiked to $1,560 from $1,500) Q2 was a modest beat/raise. Investors are now focused on share gains; the next ~12 months will be rich with announcements in short-form + sports/live, the research firm said.

While some expense timing likely benefited Q2, WF thinks NFLX has strong operating leverage.

The FY'25 revenue guide was raised from $43.5-$44.5 billion to $44.8-$45.2 billion—mostly on a weaker USD, but also on stronger sub growth + ARM (incl. ads). The '25 margin guide is +50 bps F/X neutral to 29.5%, or ~30% as reported (street 29.7%), Wells Fargo said.

Evercore ISI (reiterate "outperform"; PT hiked to $1,375 from $1,350) "Upside was expected, and upside was delivered, thanks to FX, strong subscriber growth, and ad revenue ramping. Fundamentals were impressive, with ex-FX revenue growth accelerating to 17% Y/Y and operating margin reaching a record high of 34%, thanks largely to content cost leverage. Netflix is winning the streaming market thanks to excellent execution, a stellar content slate, and scale advantages," the research firm said.

Bank of America (reiterate "buy"; PT unchanged at $1,490) "Netflix remains among the best-positioned companies in media and entertainment with sustainable growth drivers that should prove to be predictable and defensive amid a wide range of macroeconomic scenarios… Notably, growth across regions was broad-based, with each region posting double-digit FX-neutral increases," BofA said.

BofA said Netflix shares will be fueled by continued positive subscriber and earnings momentum in addition to evolving advertising and live opportunities. Supported by its world-class brand, leading global subscriber scale, position as an innovator, and increased visibility in growth drivers, the research firm believes that Netflix will continue to outperform.

Seeking Alpha analysis

Max Greve (rating "hold"): "Netflix, Inc.'s latest quarter impressed me, with strong revenue growth in UCAN and growth that went far beyond just currency tailwinds. Subscriber growth and retention remain robust, even after price hikes, and with paid sharing and advertising still evolving," Greve said on Friday.

"I'm cautious about Netflix's push into advertising and sports, fearing these could undermine its core value proposition. Comcast's announcements on the same day illustrate just how quickly a sports-ads strategy can spiral when costs are not managed appropriately. Despite a strong quarter, I maintain a Hold rating on NFLX stock due to concerns about the sustainability of the new strategic direction."

Livy Investment Research (rating "hold"): "Netflix has delivered a robust Q2 beat and raise, yet the stock took a downturn during late trading. A deeper dive into management's disclosures shows Netflix's better-than-expected outlook is primarily driven by improved FX conditions on the weaker dollar, rather than incremental outperformance from operations, the SA analyst said on Friday.

"This leaves limited mitigation against a tougher 2H24 compare. Despite the larger content slate in 2H25, it'll likely be difficult to match robust engagement acquired from prior-year events. Taken together, the set-up leaves limited durability against looming tariff-driven macro risks as well, to which Netflix's consumer-focused business model remains vulnerable to."

EquityDuo Insights (rating "sell") "NFLX remains the global leader in streaming with over 300 million subscribers and diverse content offerings. Strategic moves like cracking down on account sharing and introducing ad-supported plans have boosted subscriber growth and profitability," the SA analyst said on Friday.

"Despite robust financials, Netflix’s valuation appears stretched based on both DCF and technical analysis, suggesting limited upside potential. Given industry uncertainty, we believe Netflix stock is currently less compelling for new bullish investors."

data.title: Cohen & Steers outlines 7-8% G&A increase in 2025 guidance amid active ETF expansion and pipeline rebound
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohen & Steers (CNS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Martin Harvey stated that "our relative investment performance is strong. Fee rates are stable. Our asset classes market performance range from slightly negative for U.S. REITs to in line with market for international REITs and infrastructure strategies. Our flows turned slightly negative after 3 quarters of inflows. Our one unfunded pipeline has built back up, and we made good progress with our growth initiatives."
  • Harvey reported net outflows of $131 million in the second quarter, following three consecutive quarters of inflows, but noted positive year-to-date flows and continued market share expansion in U.S. and global real estate and infrastructure categories, as well as steady performance in preferreds.
  • The CEO highlighted the successful launch of active ETFs, with $54 million in net inflows and total AUM of $133 million, and indicated plans to "launch more active ETFs in the coming months."
  • Raja Adnan Dakkuri, CFO, stated: "Yesterday, we reported earnings of $0.73 per share compared to $0.75 sequentially. Revenue for Q2 increased 1.1% from the prior quarter to $135 million. The change in revenue from the prior quarter was driven by a few items, including higher average AUM and day count. Our effective fee rate was 59 basis points, which was in line with the prior quarter. Our operating margin was 33.6% compared to 34.7% in the prior quarter."
  • Dakkuri also said, "We expect full year G&A to increase in the 7% to 8% range as compared to full year 2024. The change in G&A is primarily driven by talent acquisition costs during 2025 as well as travel and other business development activities. Also impacting G&A are expenses related to our active ETF launch."
  • President and CIO Jon Young Cheigh noted, "The second quarter saw 89% of our AUM outperform its benchmark. On a 1-year basis, 94% of our AUM has outperformed its benchmark, while our 3-, 5- and 10-year outperformance rates are all above 95%, highlighted by 99% of AUM outperforming over 10 years."
  • Cheigh highlighted the mid-May launch of a tactical listed and private real estate strategy in partnership with IDR Investment Management, aiming to integrate listed and private real estate allocations for institutional investors.

Outlook

  • CFO Dakkuri stated: "With respect to comp and benefits for 2025, we expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40.5%, in line with our prior guidance. We expect full year G&A to increase in the 7% to 8% range as compared to full year 2024."
  • Dakkuri added, "After this year, we expect annual G&A changes to moderate from 2025 growth levels to being in the mid-single-digit percentage range."
  • Dakkuri confirmed, "We expect our effective tax rate to remain at 25.3% on an as-adjusted basis for 2025."

Financial Results

  • CFO Dakkuri reported, "Earnings of $0.73 per share compared to $0.75 sequentially. Revenue for Q2 increased 1.1% from the prior quarter to $135 million."
  • Dakkuri said, "Our operating margin was 33.6% compared to 34.7% in the prior quarter."
  • Ending AUM increased to $88.9 billion as of Q2 compared to $87.6 billion at prior quarter end, with market appreciation offsetting the impact of April's market events.
  • Dakkuri stated, "Net inflows into our open-end funds were offset by institutional net outflows. Our open-end funds have experienced positive net flows in the last 4 consecutive quarters."
  • Total expenses during Q2 were 2.9% higher than the prior quarter, driven by compensation, distribution, service fees, and G&A increases linked to business development and talent acquisition, especially for sales and distribution.
  • Liquidity at quarter end was $323 million, up from $295 million in the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • John Joseph Dunn, Evercore ISI, asked about the wealth management channel, gross sales, favored strategies, and expected seasonality. Harvey responded that "the wealth channel is very important for us and as a percentage of our AUM... We've continued to be positive in wealth. They've been a little bit less in the second quarter. If you look at our gross sales in the second quarter, they've been lower, about 10% lower than what they've recently been. But I do think there's some seasonality to that. We've had a dip in the second quarter in the past 3 to 4 years."
  • Dunn followed up on active ETFs, asking about investor profile and early marketing results. Harvey replied, "We feel like we're off to a very good start, and we can see the flows starting to build. They're still relatively small, but the people that we have brought in from other firms who have done these launches before are very excited about what we have."
  • Rodrigo Beisbier Ferreira, BofA Securities, asked about outflows in global listed infrastructure and strategy outlook. Harvey explained, "We had 2 relatively large redemptions from institutional investors who have had very large allocations to infrastructure, and they pared back some of those weightings closer to their target levels... But longer term, they're still a client, and we'll be looking forward to them reallocating at some point."
  • Ferreira asked about demand shifts in global real estate. Harvey stated, "We have had some flows into global strategies... We've seen very little reverberations from all of the policy questions around things like the revenge tax."
  • Dunn asked about geographical demand and U.S. advisory dynamics. Harvey said, "U.S. continues to be the largest and most active market. But we have burgeoning activity in Asia. I'd say Europe is a little bit slower. And the Middle East... is less active right now, but there's still opportunities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone focused on granular trends in flows and product launches, with neutral to slightly cautious sentiment, especially regarding infrastructure outflows and seasonality in the wealth channel.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting innovation, strong relative performance, and pipeline recovery. In Q&A, Harvey acknowledged challenges in institutional flows and seasonality but remained upbeat about new initiatives and long-term prospects, with phrases like "we feel like we're off to a very good start" and "we look forward to them reallocating at some point."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' questions remained pragmatic, with less focus on macroeconomic risks and more emphasis on flows and strategy uptake. Management tone was similarly confident, but with greater emphasis on execution and pipeline momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 saw a return to net outflows of $131 million after three quarters of inflows, contrasting with Q1's $222 million net inflow, but year-to-date flows remained positive.
  • Revenue increased slightly to $135 million from $133.8 million in Q1; earnings per share declined to $0.73 from $0.75.
  • Operating margin decreased to 33.6% from 34.7% in Q1.
  • The unfunded pipeline rebounded to $776 million from $61 million in Q1, reflecting renewed business activity and mandate wins.
  • Management's tone remained optimistic, now placing more emphasis on active ETF expansion and pipeline growth, while Q1 commentary focused more on macroeconomic risks and innovation.
  • Analysts' questions in Q2 focused more on specifics of product demand and regional flow dynamics, compared to Q1's broader macroeconomic and pipeline concerns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited market events in April as negatively impacting average AUM during Q2, though recovery occurred by quarter end.
  • Institutional net outflows offset gains in open-end funds, with specific reference to large redemptions in preferred securities and infrastructure strategies.
  • Management noted prospective redemptions of $500 million, with the net pipeline after redemptions at $275 million.
  • G&A expenses are expected to increase primarily due to talent acquisition and business development activities, with moderation anticipated in future years.
  • No performance-related redemptions were cited; most were attributed to tactical allocation adjustments and changes in client strategies.

Final Takeaway

Cohen & Steers reported stable fee rates, strong long-term investment performance, and a rebound in its unfunded pipeline despite a return to net outflows in the second quarter. The company’s ongoing expansion into active ETFs and new real estate strategies, alongside disciplined expense management and a positive outlook on real asset valuations, underscore its focus on growth and innovation in the evolving investment landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bullish or Bearish? SA analysts weigh in on what's next for Wall Street
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages continue their upward trajectory in July, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) reaching fresh all-time highs. Meanwhile, the Dow (DJI) remains just shy of its own record, trailing by less than 2%.

Despite the ongoing rally, an air of caution persists among investors. Concerns around tariffs, inflation, stretched valuations, interest rate policy, and geopolitical tensions continue to cast a shadow over the market’s longer-term outlook. 

As equities push higher, the central question becomes: Will the momentum continue, or is a pullback looming? To shed light on what could be next for markets, Seeking Alpha analysts have weighed in with their insights and expectations.

SA analyst Michael James McDonald said: “We believe the current S&P 500 rally is part of a larger correction, not the start of a new bull market… Divergences between the S&P 500 and equal-weighted indices, plus weak breadth, support our view of a distribution top, not a new bull market,” in Are We Still In A Bull Market?

SA Analyst Lawrence Fuller added: “Economic data remains strong with low unemployment claims and rising industrial production, supporting continued stock market strength,” in Consumers Continue To Power The Economy. 

SA Analyst Damir Tokic stated: “In my opinion, the S&P 500 is potentially topping at the 6300 level, and the downside could be significant, given the bubble-like valuation level (Shiller PE above 38)… The market had a negative reaction to a mostly positive CPI report, which suggests that investors are getting worried about the expected tariff-related inflation. ” in S&P 500: The Rally Tops At 6300. 

SA analyst Erik Conley highlighted: “I expect the market’s positive momentum to continue short-term, but gains for 2025 will likely be modest at 6-7%... For the next few weeks or so, I expect the momentum of this market to stay positive. The economy is chugging along, albeit at a slowing pace. Inflation seems to be inching higher but not running away,” in Where The Market May Be Headed Next. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).
